text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Shah 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Shah, 

Mr Alan Peters, DOB: 22-08-1986, Hospital No: K8475201 NHS No. 9431127086  
Flat 4, 12 Mariners Close, Aberdeen, AB11 5QJ  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; current pattern reported as brief bursts occurring roughly once a month, typically soon after waking 

Aetiology: 

Unclear despite prior routine EEG and MRI brain reported as within normal limits 

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild gastro-oesophageal reflux disease managed with intermittent omeprazole; seasonal allergic rhinitis 

Management Plan: 

- Continue current Levetiracetam at 1 g twice daily. Patient reports good tolerability without mood disturbance.
- Rescue plan discussed for early-morning episodes; midazolam buccal 5 mg prescribed for clusters lasting over 10 minutes or repeated spells within one hour; partner trained and written guidance provided.
- Safety advice reiterated regarding offshore duties: avoid lone work at height or in confined spaces for 24 hours following an event; ensure colleagues aware of rescue plan; maintain regular sleep schedule on rotation.
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFTs; arrange trough Levetiracetam level prior to next onshore review as a baseline only.
- Seizure triggers diary to continue, with emphasis on sleep pattern and shift changes.
- DVLA guidance reviewed; patient confirms no driving at present and understands to notify if any change.
- Follow-up: telephone review scheduled to coincide with next onshore week in eight weeks; earlier contact via epilepsy nurse specialist if event frequency escalates or new features arise (e.g. prolonged confusion, injury, or events during wakeful daytime activity).

This patient works offshore on a two-weeks-on, three-weeks-off rotation. He reports a consistent pattern over the last three months of short runs of events approximately monthly, most often noted within the first hour after awakening on rest days following a change in shift while offshore. He describes brief loss of awareness with automatisms lasting under one minute, with rapid recovery and no injuries. No episodes at depth or during active lifting tasks. Sleep disruption and early starts preceding events were noted. He has had no emergency department attendances and no status episodes. Adherence to medication is good, with alarms set on his phone and dosing supervised when on the rig.

We agreed to continue current therapy, emphasising sleep hygiene and structured handovers to minimise abrupt shift transitions offshore. He will provide rig occupational health with the rescue protocol and contact details for our team. No immediate changes to medication are proposed, pending the seizure diary review and baseline levels at the next onshore appointment.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: current pattern reported as brief bursts occurring roughly once a month

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per month, multiple per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient’s current pattern as “brief bursts occurring roughly once a month” and later as “short runs of events approximately monthly,” indicating clustered events. The number of seizures within each burst/run is not specified. Therefore, infer a cluster frequency of about 1 per month, with an unspecified (multiple) number per cluster. No other higher-frequency figure is provided.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per month, multiple per cluster
current pattern reported as brief bursts occurring roughly once a month
reference: 1 cluster per month, multiple per cluster
